preFix.title: Applying a render scratch coat to exterior brickwork
preFix.subtitle: Prepare a backing coat before the finish render or dash coat
preFix.excerpt: How to apply a scratch coat of sand-and-cement render to exterior brickwork, including gauging the mix ratio, applying and levelling with a feather-edge rule, scratching the surface for key, and managing curing in different weather conditions.
preFix.sourceNotes: British Standard BS 5262:2011 External Renderings. Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Plastering syllabus.

content.text: A
scratch coat

is the first working coat of sand-and-cement render, applied to bare masonry before the finish coat. It bridges surface irregularities in the brickwork and provides a keyed surface for the finish coat to bond to. Without it, the finish coat will delaminate: either at once or under the first frost. This rates intermediate because it needs mixing judgment, speed while the mortar is workable, and awareness of how weather affects curing.
Step 1, prepare the wall surface

Remove any loose material from the brickwork with a stiff wire brush. Old limewash or paint must be scraped and wire-brushed back to sound material: render will not bond to a flaking surface. Check the brickwork joints: any that are hollow or crumbling must be raked back 15 mm and repointed before rendering. Hose the wall down and allow the surface to reach a uniformly damp but not wet state: this controls how fast the mortar dries during application.
Dubbing out
is the next step: any hollows greater than 10 mm deep should be filled with a stiff mortar (1:3 cement-to-sharp-sand mix) and left to harden for at least 24 hours before the scratch coat is applied.
What you will need: materials

Ordinary Portland cement. Sharp sand (grit sand), not soft building sand. The standard scratch-coat mix for brick is 1 part cement to 4 parts sharp sand by volume, sometimes written 1:4. A plasticiser or a small quantity of lime (half a part) improves workability and reduces shrinkage cracking: either is acceptable. Mix in a clean drum or on a clean board; never mix on contaminated ground where soil or organic matter can enter. You will also need galvanised expanded-metal lathing (EML) or bead stops at any change of substrate and stainless-steel stop beads or angle beads to define the edges and corners.
Step 2, set up screeds or depth guides

Fix stainless-steel depth beads vertically down the wall at 1200 mm intervals using dabs of the scratch-coat mix. The beads establish the finished depth of the scratch coat: typically 10 to 12 mm for brick, thicker if the wall is very uneven. Check the beads are plumb with the spirit level before the dabs harden. Once the beads are set, the
feather-edge rule
will rule off the applied render to the face of the beads.
Step 3, apply the scratch coat

Load the hawk with a good charge of mix. Throw the mortar onto the wall with the trowel from the hawk, working from the bottom of the section upwards. Press firmly to ensure contact with every brick face and joint. Once a section between two screeds is covered, rule off the surface by drawing the feather-edge rule up the wall in a sawing motion, resting on both beads. The rule takes the high spots off; any hollows should be filled with additional mortar and ruled off again.
Step 4, scratch the surface

When the scratch coat has firmed sufficiently (it should hold a thumb impression without crumbling: typically 30 to 60 minutes in normal conditions), scratch the entire surface in a wavy horizontal pattern with a comb scratcher or a piece of timber fitted with nails at 25 mm centres. The scratches should be 3 to 5 mm deep, consistent, and cover the whole face. The key that these scratches provide is the bond for the finish coat: do not skip or rush this step.
Step 5, cure the scratch coat

Allow the scratch coat to cure for at least three days: five in cool weather: before applying the finish coat. During hot or windy weather, mist the scratch coat with water twice daily for the first 48 hours to slow the cure and prevent shrinkage cracking. In cold weather (below 5°C), do not apply render: the cement will not hydrate properly and the coat will be weak. If a frost arrives after application, protect with hessian sacking or render protection sheeting until temperatures rise above 5°C.
Variations

On a smooth concrete block background (denser than brick and less absorbent), the surface should be pre-treated with a bonding agent before the scratch coat to guarantee adhesion. On timber-framed walls, render must go over stainless-steel EML fixed to a breather membrane: the render does not bond to timber directly. A two-coat system (scratch coat plus finish coat) is the domestic standard; for exposed locations, three coats with a base coat, middle coat, and finish are used.

attrs.fix: Hack off all loose material. Clean, dampen the surface, apply a slurry coat of cement and water, let it dry slightly, then reapply the scratch coat. Use bonding agent on smooth or low-suction backgrounds.
attrs.cause: Wall was too dry or too smooth when applied; insufficient bonding agent on dense block.
attrs.symptom: Scratch coat separates from brickwork in sheets

attrs.fix: Shallow map cracking that does not penetrate the full coat can be left — the finish coat will bridge it if bonded properly. Deep cracking must be hacked off and reapplied in two thinner passes, curing each before the next.
attrs.cause: Mix was too rich in cement; coat applied too thick in one pass; the surface dried too fast in hot or windy conditions.
attrs.symptom: Map cracking across the scratch coat surface within 48 hours

attrs.fix: Let the mix stiffen slightly before reapplying. Apply in no more than 12 mm per coat. Allow the wall surface to reach a damp-not-wet state before throwing on mortar.
attrs.cause: Mix too wet; too much mortar applied in one pass; wall surface too wet.
attrs.symptom: Render slumps off the wall during application
